column7: Previous studies have established that physical confinement accurately mimics the mechanical stress and spatial restrictions cancer cells experience during tissue invasion. Devices utilizing polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) spacers can precisely dictate the height of the space cells occupy in vitro, applying controlled mechanical forces directly to the cell and its nucleus. It is an established biological principle that mechanical deformation of the nucleus under such confinement stretches the nuclear envelope, which can alter chromatin organization, trigger mechanotransduction pathways, and induce sustained changes in gene expression.
column8: we subjected cultured human melanoma cells to two-dimensional mechanical confinement using a custom PDMS-based cell confiner device set to a restrictive height of 3 μm to force nuclear deformation. After a predefined period of confinement, we released the cells and performed RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) to assess stable transcriptomic alterations, followed by in vitro Boyden chamber transwell assays to evaluate subsequent changes in their invasive capacity.
column9: It is well established that mechanical forces from the microenvironment can influence nuclear architecture and gene expression through mechanotransduction pathways. For example, physical deformation of the nucleus has been shown to alter chromatin organization and accessibility, leading to persistent transcriptional changes. Studies using confined migration assays and engineered substrates have demonstrated that mechanical stress can induce epigenetic remodeling and stable phenotypic transitions in cancer cells. Additionally, prior work has shown that substrate stiffness and confinement can regulate YAP/TAZ signaling and chromatin state, linking physical cues to long-term cellular behavior. These findings support the concept that mechanical inputs can drive durable changes in gene expression programs in tumour cells.
column10: culture melanoma cells in microfabricated confinement devices or dense extracellular matrix gels to impose controlled mechanical compression, followed by ATAC-seq and RNA-seq to assess changes in chromatin accessibility and gene expression, and validate phenotypic switching using invasion assays and immunostaining for proliferative versus invasive markers
column11: Melanoma cells exhibit significant plasticity, allowing them to transition between transcriptomic and phenotypic states in response to cues from the tumour microenvironment rather than relying solely on genetic mutations [1]. Scientific research has established that mechanical confinement specifically induces stable changes in chromatin architecture, which can reprogram cells from a proliferative to an invasive state (DOI: 10.1038/s41556-023-01314-1).
column12: we will employ a combination of zebrafish transgenics and human melanoma samples to observe and measure the induction of stable changes in chromatin architecture and phenotypic transitions under conditions of mechanical confinement [1].
